mnemonics.title: RE Process Steps
mnemonics.content: FEAST - Feasibility, Elicitation, Analysis, Specification, Validation, Management (6 steps)

mnemonics.title: Types of Requirements
mnemonics.content: FUN - Functional, Non-functional, User requirements (3 main types)

mnemonics.title: SRS Quality Attributes
mnemonics.content: CRISP - Complete, Relevant, Implementable, Specific, Precise (properties of good SRS)

mnemonics.title: Requirements Elicitation Techniques
mnemonics.content: IBJPO - Interview, Brainstorming, Joint Application Development, Prototyping, Observation (main techniques)

mnemonics.title: Non-Functional Requirements Categories
mnemonics.content: PURSED - Performance, Usability, Reliability, Security, Efficiency, Maintainability, Data Integrity (categories)

mnemonics.title: User Story Format
mnemonics.content: AAA - As a (role), Action (want to do), Advantage (benefit received)

mnemonics.title: Stakeholder Categories
mnemonics.content: CUDOS - Customers, Users, Developers, Operators, Stakeholders (interested parties)

acronyms.usage: The discipline of identifying, documenting, and managing system requirements
The official document describing complete system requirements
Quality attributes like performance, security, reliability
Features and functions the system must perform
Standard language for visualizing and modeling requirements
Collaborative requirements gathering technique with stakeholders

acronyms.expansion: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers
acronyms.usage: Organization that sets standards including SRS guidelines (IEEE 830)

acronyms.expansion: Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have

visualTips.visualization: Think of requirements as a pyramid: Business requirements (top) → User requirements → System requirements → Functional & Non-functional requirements (base)
Draw actors as stick figures outside a boundary (system), connect with lines to use cases (ovals) showing what actors can do with the system
Three columns: WHO (user role), WHAT (goal), WHY (benefit) - like a story card with character, plot, and resolution
Circle of verification: Requirements → Review → Test Design → Test Execution → Validation → Changes → Updated Requirements
Grid/table linking requirements ID to source, design components, code modules, and test cases - like a connected network diagram

analogies.analogy: Like an architect drawing blueprints before building a house - without clear plans, the building will have problems
Like a restaurant menu - lists all the dishes (features) the kitchen can prepare for customers
Like restaurant ambiance, service speed, and cleanliness - quality aspects that affect the experience but aren't the food itself
Like a doctor diagnosing a patient - asking questions, observing symptoms, gathering information before prescribing treatment
Like a model car before mass production - shows general shape and features to validate design before full manufacturing
Like a legal contract between client and builder - clearly specifies what will be built, how it will work, and constraints
Like a product's journey from raw material to finished product - every step can be traced back to source
